english: the right thigh Glr., brla-rkaṅ (brla rkang) femoral bone (Sch.: hip-bone?). *brla-kuṅ* (*brla kung*) groin W.; brla-bar (brla bar) Sch.: junction of the legs, genitals; brla-rús (brla rus) femoral bone; brla-śá (brla sha) muscular part of the thigh; brla-súl (brla sul) Cs.: 'side of the thigh'.

Lex. and Sch. abusive word, invective, abusive language (Sch. also: 'rude fellow, brute'?), rtsub-brlaṅ-ba ma yín-pa (rtsub brlang ba ma yin pa) refraining from abusive language Thgy.; brlaṅ-po-rnams byéd-pa (brlang po rnams byed pa) to make use of such language Stg.; brlaṅ-spyód byéd-pa (brlang spyod byed pa) to be coarse, churlish Sch.

C. firm, secure, safe (Sch.: quick?), brliṅ-po (brling po) id., both of men and things, *liṅ-ghyi*)he-la kur* (*ling ghyi*)he la khur*) C. carry it safely, carefully! brliṅ-lóg (brling log) Sch.: confused, disorderly, not to be trusted.
Sch.: = mdzá-bo (mdza' bo) friend, assistant, helper; one Lex. explains bló-brlug (blo brlug) by grogs (grogs).

I. sbst. mountain pass, road or passage over a mountain, laí gyen (la'i gyen) the up-hill road or ascent of a mountain, laí t'ur (la'i thur) the down-hill road or descent Cs.; la rgál-ba (la rgal ba) B., C. (W.: *gyáb-ce* (*gyab ce*)) to cross a mountain pass; lá-la °gró-ba (la la 'gro ba) Cs. id. Comp. la-rkéd (la rked) or skéd (sked) the declivity or slope of a mountain pass. — la-k'á (la kha) the high-est point of the pass, la-mgó (la mgo) the head, or top, of a mountain pass. — la-sgó (la sgo), Sch.: 'turnpike of a pass'. — la-yčáṅ-pa (la gcang pa) a collec-tor of duties on a ghat or pass Cs. — la-čúṅ (la chung) a small pass Glr. — la-mjúg (la mjug) = la-rtsa (la rtsa). la-stóṅ (la stong) v. stóṅ-pa (stong pa). — la-tóg (la tog) = la-rtsé (la rtse). — la-rtsá (la rtsa) (W. *lar-sa* (*lar sa*)) foot of a mountain pass la-rtsé (la rtse) (W. *lar-sé* (*lar se*)) top of it. — la-śáṅ (la shang) Sch.: = la-rkéd (la rked). II. sbst., also lá-ba (la ba), wax-light, wax-candle, taper, from the Chinese láh wax, C. III. In compounds for la-púg (la phug) and la-čá (la cha). IV. postpos. c. acc. 1. denoting local relations in quite a general sense, in answer to the questions where and whither: sá-la °gré-ba (sa la 'gre ba) to roll (one's body) on the ground, sá-la °gril-ba (sa la 'gril ba) to fall down on the ground, nám-mka-la °p'ág-pa (nam mkha' la 'phag pa) to rise to heaven, nám-mka-la °p'úr-ba (nam mkha' la 'phur ba) to fly in the air, mé-la (me la) at, on, in, to, the fire, ri-la (ri la) on, to, the moun-tain, čú-la (chu la) in, into, to, on, the water, śár-la (shar la) to, towards the east, eastward (e.g. to look), bód-la (bod la) in, to, Tibet; also where we should say: from, as: ynám-la k'á-ba °bab (gnam la kha ba 'bab) snow falls from heaven, rtá-la °báb (rta la 'bab) he alights from his horse, brág-la mčóṅs (brag la mchongs) he leaps down from the rock Dzl., lús-la krag °byín-pa (lus la khrag 'byin pa) to draw blood from the body by scratching. This latter use of la (la) occurs so frequently, that it cannot always be looked upon as a misspelling for las (las), though this would be the more exact word. — 2. with reference to time: žag ysúm-pa-la (zhag gsum pa la) on the third day, lo nyí-śú-pa-la (lo nyi shu pa la) in the twentieth or during the twentieth year, zlá-ba ysúm-la (zla ba gsum la) (finish it) within three months Glr., p'yag dáṅ-po-la (phyag dang po la) at, during, the first obeisance Glr. — 3. in other bearings: dé-la rtén-nas (de la rten nas) (prop. relying
